required.title: [For Mentor] TED Talks: The Official TED Guide to Public Speaking
required.author: Chris Anderson

suggested.title: [Watch and Analyze] Great speeches for young audiences
[For Mentor] Competitive Debate: The Official Guide
suggested.author: Richard Edwards

supplementary.title: [Read] Selected speeches from Lend Me Your Ears: Great Speeches in History
supplementary.author: edited by William Safire

supplementary.title: [Join] A speech and debate club, Toastmasters Youth Program, or homeschool debate league

competence_markers.description: The student can deliver a five-minute prepared speech with clear opening-body-conclusion structure, minimal filler words, and effective delivery
The student can deliver a two-minute impromptu speech on a random topic using the PREP method with coherent structure
The student demonstrates deliberate use of pace, pause, and volume variation in speeches
The student can participate in a formal debate, constructing a case, cross-examining, and rebutting
The student can argue a position they personally disagree with, making the strongest possible case for it
The student can analyze a published speech, identifying its structure, rhetorical techniques, and delivery choices
The student uses specific techniques to manage nervousness and can articulate what works for them
The student has delivered at least two speeches to unfamiliar audiences outside their regular group
Filler word frequency has measurably decreased from the beginning to the end of the packet

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain the structure of a good speech.
narration_prompts.detail: What goes in the opening? The body? The conclusion? Why does this structure work?

narration_prompts.prompt: Give me an impromptu speech on this topic: [random topic].
narration_prompts.detail: You have 30 seconds to prepare. Use the PREP method.

narration_prompts.prompt: What are filler words, and why do they weaken a speech?
narration_prompts.detail: How many fillers did you have in your last recording? What are you doing to reduce them?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe the difference between persuasive and informative speaking.
narration_prompts.detail: When would you use each one? Give an example of a topic suited to each.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a debate you participated in.
narration_prompts.detail: What was your position? What was the strongest argument the other side made? How did you respond to it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does nervousness feel like in your body before you speak?
narration_prompts.detail: What techniques help you manage it? Which one works best for you?

cross_connections.description: Public speaking is the delivery vehicle; rhetoric provides the argument structure. 3.3.6 builds how you say it; 3.3.9 builds what you say and why it is logically compelling. Together they produce the complete persuasive speaker.

Delivery is physical. Posture, gesture, eye contact, and composed movement are the nonverbal channel that either reinforces or contradicts your words. Every Weathering Storms speaking exercise is simultaneously a 3.3.1 exercise.
A great speech delivered to the wrong audience at the wrong time fails. Social calibration determines when to speak, how to read the audience, and how to adjust in real time.
Essay structure and speech structure are parallel architectures. The student who writes well-organized essays writes well-organized speeches, and vice versa.

Debate trains the student to detect weak arguments — their own and others'. Cross-examination is applied critical thinking. The defensive logic of 1.7 and the offensive argumentation of debate are two faces of the same skill.
Every speech, every impromptu exercise, every debate is a controlled stress exposure. The student who has given fifty speeches under pressure has a resilience that extends far beyond the podium.

content.why_this_matters:
At Stretching Branches, you learned to stand up and speak without fear. That was the foundation. Now you are going to learn to speak with structure, with purpose, and under pressure — including when you have no time to prepare.

Most real-world speaking is impromptu. Someone asks your opinion in a meeting. A teacher calls on you. A friend asks you to explain something. A group needs someone to speak up and no one is volunteering. In all of these moments, the person who can organize their thoughts quickly and deliver them clearly has an advantage that the person who freezes or rambles does not.

You will also learn the formal structure of a speech — the architecture that makes five minutes of talking feel purposeful instead of random. You will learn to debate, arguing positions you may not even agree with, because the ability to argue any side of a question is one of the most powerful thinking tools that exists. And you will learn to manage the nervousness that still comes — not by eliminating it, but by channeling it into energy that makes your speaking better.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Formal speech structure** — the five-paragraph speech: opening, three supporting points, conclusion
2. **Impromptu speaking ability** — organizing and delivering coherent thoughts with little or no preparation
3. **Nervousness management** — techniques for channeling anxiety into effective performance
4. **Vocal delivery refinement** — eliminating filler words, using pace and pause deliberately, projecting with warmth or authority
5. **Eye contact as connection** — engaging individuals in an audience, not scanning or staring
6. **Debate fundamentals** — constructing cases, rebutting arguments, cross-examining, arguing positions you disagree with
7. **Persuasive versus informative speaking** — understanding when to teach and when to convince
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Architecture of a Speech

Every effective speech — whether it is two minutes or twenty — has the same basic architecture:

**Opening (Hook + Thesis)**
Your opening does two things: it captures attention (the hook) and it tells the audience what your speech is about (the thesis).

Hooks that work:
- A surprising fact: "Every year, Americans throw away enough food to fill 730 football stadiums."
- A question: "When was the last time you changed your mind about something important?"
- A story: "Last March, I stood in front of a judge and argued a case I thought I would lose."
- A bold statement: "Most of what you learned about the American Revolution is wrong."

Your thesis is one clear sentence that states your main point: "Today I am going to show you why food waste is a solvable problem." "I want to convince you that changing your mind is a sign of strength, not weakness."

**Body (Three Supporting Points)**
Each supporting point develops your thesis with evidence, examples, or stories. Each point follows its own mini-structure:
- State the point
- Provide evidence or an example
- Explain how this supports your thesis
- Transition to the next point

Three points is not a magic number, but it works. Two feels thin. Four starts to lose the audience. Three gives you enough depth without overstaying.

**Conclusion (Restatement + Final Thought)**
Restate your thesis in slightly different words. Then end with something memorable — a call to action, a final image, a return to your opening story, or a question for the audience to carry with them.

The conclusion is where most speakers fail. They trail off: "So, yeah, that's basically what I wanted to talk about..." A strong conclusion is decisive. You say your final sentence. You pause. You sit down. The audience knows it is over because you landed, not because you ran out of things to say.

### Part 2: Impromptu Speaking — Thinking on Your Feet

Impromptu speaking is speaking on a topic with little or no preparation time. It is the most practical speaking skill you will develop, because most real speaking is impromptu.

**The PREP Method** — a simple structure for organizing impromptu remarks in 30 seconds:
- **P**oint — state your main idea
- **R**eason — explain why
- **E**xample — give a specific example
- **P**oint — restate your main idea

Example prompt: "Should students have homework?"
- Point: "I believe homework should be limited to meaningful practice, not busywork."
- Reason: "Because students need time to read, play, and explore on their own, and busywork does not actually improve learning."
- Example: "Last year, I spent an hour every night on worksheets that repeated what I already knew. That time could have been spent reading a book I chose or working on a project I cared about."
- Point: "Homework should exist to deepen learning, not to fill time."

That is a complete impromptu response — clear, structured, specific — and it took less than a minute. Practice this structure until it is automatic. When someone asks your opinion on anything, PREP gives you a framework to respond coherently instead of rambling.

### Part 3: Managing Nervousness

Nervousness before speaking is not a problem. It is energy. Your body is preparing you for a high-attention situation by increasing your heart rate, sharpening your focus, and flooding you with adrenaline. Elite athletes experience the same thing before a competition. The difference between a nervous wreck and a confident speaker is not the presence or absence of nervousness — it is what you do with it.

**Techniques that work:**

1. **Reframe:** Instead of "I'm scared," say "I'm ready." The physical sensations of fear and excitement are identical. Your brain believes the label you give it.

2. **Breathe:** Box breathing — inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Three cycles. This engages your parasympathetic nervous system and lowers your heart rate. Do it before you stand up.

3. **Focus outward:** Nervousness is self-focus: "How do I look? What if I forget? What if they judge me?" Shift focus to the audience: "What do they need to hear? How can I help them understand this?" When you focus on serving the audience instead of protecting yourself, the nervousness diminishes.

4. **Prepare more than you think you need to.** The number one cause of speaking anxiety is under-preparation. The speaker who knows their material cold has one less thing to worry about.

5. **Expose yourself repeatedly.** Nervousness decreases with repetition. Your tenth speech is less terrifying than your first. Your fiftieth is routine. There is no shortcut except doing it.

### Part 4: Vocal Delivery

Your voice is an instrument. At Stretching Branches, you learned to play it at sufficient volume. Now you learn to play it with nuance.

**Filler words:** "Um," "uh," "like," "you know," "so," "basically." These words fill silence when you are thinking. They weaken your message. Record yourself speaking for two minutes and count your fillers. Most people are shocked by the number. The cure: replace fillers with silence. A pause where "um" would have been is not awkward — it is powerful. It signals confidence. Practice pausing instead of filling.

**Pace:** Most nervous speakers talk too fast. Slow down. A good speaking pace is slower than normal conversation. Vary your pace: faster for excitement or building energy, slower for important points. The slowest sentence in your speech should be the most important one.

**Pause:** The most powerful vocal tool. A pause after a key statement forces the audience to absorb it. A pause before a key statement creates anticipation. "The number of children in this country who go to bed hungry every night is... [pause]... one in six." The pause makes the number land.

**Volume:** Match volume to room and purpose. You should be comfortably heard in the back row without shouting. Lower your voice for emphasis on intimate or serious points — this pulls the audience in. Raise it for emphasis on calls to action or moments of high energy.

### Part 5: Debate — Arguing Both Sides

Debate is the most powerful speaking training available. Here is why: it forces you to argue positions you may disagree with, which teaches you something no other exercise teaches — how to understand a position from the inside.

**Basic debate structure (simplified Lincoln-Douglas):**

1. **Affirmative constructive** (4 minutes) — The affirmative side presents their case: thesis, definitions, supporting arguments with evidence
2. **Cross-examination by negative** (2 minutes) — The negative asks questions to test the affirmative's case
3. **Negative constructive** (4 minutes) — The negative presents their case and rebuts the affirmative
4. **Cross-examination by affirmative** (2 minutes) — The affirmative asks questions
5. **Affirmative rebuttal** (3 minutes) — The affirmative responds to the negative's arguments
6. **Negative rebuttal** (3 minutes) — Final negative response
7. **Closing statements** (2 minutes each)

**Debate rules that matter:**
- Attack arguments, not people. "Your evidence is outdated" is legitimate. "You're stupid" is not.
- You must respond to what your opponent actually said, not what you wish they said.
- When cross-examining, ask questions — do not make speeches disguised as questions.
- When you are losing an argument, acknowledge the strong points of the other side and redirect to your strongest ground. Never pretend a good argument against you does not exist.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Monthly Prepared Speech [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The student writes and delivers at least one five-minute prepared speech per month using the opening-body-conclusion structure. Topics alternate between informative and persuasive. Each speech is delivered to an audience (family, co-op, community group), recorded, and reviewed.

### Study 2: Weekly Impromptu Practice [Ongoing]
At least twice per week, the student draws a random topic and delivers a one-to-two-minute impromptu response using the PREP method. Time with a stopwatch. The mentor provides feedback on structure, filler words, and clarity.

### Study 3: Filler Word Boot Camp [Early in the Packet]
Record the student speaking for two minutes on any topic. Count every filler word. Set a goal to reduce fillers by half within one month. The exercise: every time the student catches themselves about to say "um," they pause silently instead. Record weekly to track progress.

### Study 4: Speech Analysis [Monthly]
The student watches or reads a great speech and analyzes it: What is the opening hook? What is the thesis? What are the supporting points? Where does the speaker pause? Where do they change pace? What makes the conclusion effective? Written or oral analysis.

### Study 5: Debate Practice [At Least Quarterly]
The student participates in at least one formal or semiformal debate per quarter. If a debate club is available, join it. If not, the mentor and student debate a topic using the simplified structure above. The student argues both sides on different occasions.

### Study 6: The Unfamiliar Audience [At Least Twice During the Packet]
The student delivers a prepared speech to an audience of people they do not know well — a community group, a different homeschool co-op, a church gathering, a public speaking competition. Familiar-audience comfort is necessary but not sufficient.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Speech Structure
**Developing well:** Speeches have clear openings with hooks, three developed points with evidence, and decisive conclusions. Transitions between points are smooth. The audience can follow the logic.

**Developing slowly:** Speeches ramble without clear structure. Points blend into each other. Conclusions trail off. The student has material but not architecture.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student outlining before speaking? Require a written outline (not a script) for every prepared speech. Is the student hearing well-structured speeches as models?

### 2. Impromptu Ability
**Developing well:** The student can deliver a coherent one-to-two-minute response to a random topic with a discernible PREP structure. They do not freeze or ramble excessively.

**Developing slowly:** The student freezes, says "I don't know," or talks in circles without arriving at a point.

**Diagnostic questions:** How often is the student practicing? Impromptu ability requires high repetition — dozens of attempts before it becomes natural. Are the topics appropriate in difficulty?

### 3. Delivery Quality
**Developing well:** Filler words are decreasing measurably. The student uses pace, pause, and volume deliberately. Eye contact moves among audience members.

**Developing slowly:** Filler words remain frequent. Delivery is monotone or rushed. Eye contact is absent or fixated on one person.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student reviewing recordings of themselves? Self-observation is the fastest path to delivery improvement. Are you giving specific delivery feedback after every speech?

### 4. Debate Competence
**Developing well:** The student can construct a case with clear claims and evidence, cross-examine an opponent with probing questions, and rebut arguments they have not heard before. They can argue positions they disagree with.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot separate their personal opinion from an argument assignment. Cross-examination consists of statements disguised as questions. Rebuttals address what the student wished the opponent said rather than what they actually said.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been exposed to formal debate structure? Has the student practiced cross-examination as a separate skill? Is the student debating against a competent opponent who challenges them?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Scripting Instead of Structuring
The student should outline speeches, not write them word-for-word and read them aloud. A scripted speech is a reading exercise, not a speaking exercise. The outline provides structure; the delivery is extemporaneous. If the student insists on scripting, wean them off by reducing what they can bring to the podium: full script, then one page of notes, then an index card, then nothing.

### Avoiding Impromptu Practice
Impromptu speaking is uncomfortable by design. The student may resist it. Do not let them. The discomfort is the training stimulus. Make it routine and brief — one minute, twice a week — so it is a habit, not an ordeal.

### Soft Debate Opponents
If you always let the student win debates or avoid attacking their weak points, you are training them for a world that does not exist. Be a rigorous opponent. Point out logical gaps. Press on weak evidence. The student needs to experience losing an argument so they learn to strengthen their arguments.

### Neglecting Audience Variety
Monthly speeches to the same family audience produce comfort with that audience, not public speaking skill. The student needs regular exposure to unfamiliar audiences: co-op presentations, community events, competitions, or simply a different group of adults.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Speech Structure
**Strong:** Can articulate the three-part structure with specific terminology (hook, thesis, supporting points, conclusion). Can explain why structure matters — not just what it is.
**Weak:** Vague description. Cannot distinguish between the parts of a speech.

### Prompt 2: Impromptu Speech
**Strong:** Delivers a coherent response with a recognizable PREP structure within the time limit. Makes a clear point with a specific example.
**Weak:** Rambles without structure. No clear point. Exceeds time significantly or finishes in 15 seconds.

### Prompt 3: Filler Words
**Strong:** Can name their most common fillers, cite their count from a recent recording, and describe their strategy for reducing them.
**Weak:** "I don't really say um that much." (They do. Everyone does. The student who thinks they do not has not listened to a recording.)

### Prompt 5: Debate Reflection
**Strong:** Can articulate the strongest argument the other side made and explain how they responded. Shows respect for the opposing position even while disagreeing.
**Weak:** "They were wrong about everything." Cannot identify a single strong point from the opposition.
